ucs.title: [LLM] Tonto Team / CactusPete: Equation Editor (eqnedt32.exe) exploit chain spawning code execution (CVE-2017-11882 / CVE-2018-0798)
ucs.description: Hunts the canonical Tonto Team / CactusPete / Earth Akhlut spearphishing chain where weaponised RTF/DOC attachments exploit the Microsoft Equation Editor flaws (CVE-2017-11882, CVE-2018-0798) to execute code. Tonto Team has relied on these Equation Editor exploits for years to deliver Bisonal, Dexbia and ShadowPad to government/military/energy targets across South Korea, Japan, Taiwan and the US — a child process of eqnedt32.exe is virtually never benign.
ucs.splunk: | tstats summariesonly=true count min(_time) as firstTime max(_time) as lastTime values(Processes.process) as cmdline values(Processes.process_path) as child_path values(Processes.parent_process) as parent_cmdline values(Processes.user) as user from datamodel=Endpoint.Processes where Processes.parent_process_name="eqnedt32.exe" Processes.process_name IN ("cmd.exe","powershell.exe","pwsh.exe","wscript.exe","cscript.exe","mshta.exe","rundll32.exe","regsvr32.exe","certutil.exe","bitsadmin.exe","curl.exe","wget.exe","hh.exe","schtasks.exe") by Processes.dest Processes.user Processes.process_name Processes.parent_process_name _time span=1m | `drop_dm_object_name(Processes)` | convert ctime(firstTime) ctime(lastTime)
ucs.kql:
// Tonto Team / CactusPete — Equation Editor exploit child processes
// CVE-2017-11882 / CVE-2018-0798 — signature delivery vector for Bisonal RAT
let _suspicious_children = dynamic([
    "cmd.exe","powershell.exe","pwsh.exe","wscript.exe","cscript.exe",
    "mshta.exe","rundll32.exe","regsvr32.exe","certutil.exe",
    "bitsadmin.exe","curl.exe","wget.exe","hh.exe","schtasks.exe",
    "net.exe","net1.exe","reg.exe","installutil.exe"
]);
DeviceProcessEvents
| where Timestamp > ago(30d)
| where InitiatingProcessFileName =~ "eqnedt32.exe"   // Equation Editor
| where AccountName !endswith "$"
| where FileName in~ (_suspicious_children)
   or FolderPath has_any (@"\Users\Public\", @"\AppData\Local\Temp\",
                          @"\AppData\Roaming\", @"\ProgramData\")
   or ProcessCommandLine has_any ("http://","https://","-EncodedCommand",
                                   "DownloadString","DownloadFile",
                                   "FromBase64String","-nop","-w hidden")
| project Timestamp, DeviceName, AccountName,
          ParentImage = InitiatingProcessFolderPath,
          ParentCmd   = InitiatingProcessCommandLine,
          Child       = FileName,
          ChildPath   = FolderPath,
          ChildCmd    = ProcessCommandLine,
          ChildSHA256 = SHA256
| order by Timestamp desc
ucs.sentinel_kql:
// Tonto Team / CactusPete — Equation Editor exploit child processes
// Sentinel SecurityEvent (4688) port — requires command-line audit
let _suspicious_children = dynamic([
    "cmd.exe","powershell.exe","pwsh.exe","wscript.exe","cscript.exe",
    "mshta.exe","rundll32.exe","regsvr32.exe","certutil.exe",
    "bitsadmin.exe","curl.exe","wget.exe","hh.exe","schtasks.exe",
    "net.exe","net1.exe","reg.exe","installutil.exe"
]);
SecurityEvent
| where TimeGenerated > ago(30d)
| where EventID == 4688
| where Account !endswith "$"
| where ParentProcessName endswith "\\eqnedt32.exe"   // Equation Editor
| extend NewProcessLeaf = tolower(tostring(split(NewProcessName, "\\")[-1]))
| where NewProcessLeaf in (_suspicious_children)
   or NewProcessName has_any (@"\Users\Public\", @"\AppData\Local\Temp\",
                              @"\AppData\Roaming\", @"\ProgramData\")
   or CommandLine has_any ("http://","https://","-EncodedCommand",
                            "DownloadString","DownloadFile",
                            "FromBase64String","-nop","-w hidden")
| project TimeGenerated, Computer, Account,
          ParentProcessName, NewProcessName, CommandLine, TokenElevationType
| order by TimeGenerated desc
ucs.sigma_yaml:
title: Tonto Team Equation Editor Exploit Child Process
id: 7e2c4b81-3a24-4dc2-9e3a-tontoeqnedt32
status: experimental
description: Detects child processes spawned by Microsoft Equation Editor (eqnedt32.exe) — a Tonto Team / CactusPete signature delivery vector exploiting CVE-2017-11882 / CVE-2018-0798.
references:
  - https://attack.mitre.org/groups/G0131/
  - https://securelist.com/cactuspete-apt-groups-updated-bisonal-backdoor/97962/
author: SOC Detection Engineering
date: 2026/05/03
tags:
  - attack.initial_access
  - attack.execution
  - attack.t1566.001
  - attack.t1203
  - attack.t1204.002
  - attack.g0131
logsource:
  category: process_creation
  product: windows
detection:
  selection_parent:
    ParentImage|endswith: '\eqnedt32.exe'
  selection_child:
    Image|endswith:
      - '\cmd.exe'
      - '\powershell.exe'
      - '\pwsh.exe'
      - '\wscript.exe'
      - '\cscript.exe'
      - '\mshta.exe'
      - '\rundll32.exe'
      - '\regsvr32.exe'
      - '\certutil.exe'
      - '\bitsadmin.exe'
      - '\hh.exe'
      - '\schtasks.exe'
  condition: selection_parent and selection_child
falsepositives:
  - None known — eqnedt32.exe should never spawn shell or LOLBin children in normal use.
level: high
ucs.rationale: Tonto Team / CactusPete is one of the most prolific abusers of the Microsoft Equation Editor vulnerabilities (CVE-2017-11882 and CVE-2018-0798) — Kaspersky, Trend Micro and ESET all attribute multi-year Bisonal RAT campaigns delivered via weaponised RTF/DOC documents that trigger eqnedt32.exe. The Equation Editor process is essentially deprecated and has no business spawning shell/LOLBin children

_field_issues.message: `DownloadString` is not a canonical column of DeviceProcessEvents.
`DownloadFile` is not a canonical column of DeviceProcessEvents.
`FromBase64String` is not a canonical column of DeviceProcessEvents.

_sentinel_field_issues.message: `DownloadString` is not a canonical column of SecurityEvent.
`DownloadFile` is not a canonical column of SecurityEvent.
`FromBase64String` is not a canonical column of SecurityEvent.

_sigma_issues: sigma parse error: Sigma rule identifier must be an UUID

ucs.title: [LLM] Tonto Team Bisonal/ShadowPad DLL search-order hijacking via legitimate signed binary in user-writable path
ucs.description: Hunts the Tonto Team / BRONZE HUNTLEY / Karma Panda DLL side-loading pattern where a legitimate signed binary (often a Microsoft, Kaspersky, or vendor utility) is dropped to %APPDATA%, %TEMP% or %ProgramData% alongside a malicious DLL with a hijacked name — the exe is launched and pulls the adjacent attacker DLL via search order. This is the dominant persistence/execution technique for the Bisonal, Dexbia, and ShadowPad implants Tonto deploys post-exploit.
ucs.splunk: | tstats summariesonly=true count min(_time) as firstTime max(_time) as lastTime values(Filesystem.file_path) as dll_path values(Filesystem.file_name) as dll_name values(Filesystem.process_name) as loader_exe values(Filesystem.user) as user from datamodel=Endpoint.Filesystem where Filesystem.action=created Filesystem.file_name="*.dll" (Filesystem.file_path="*\\AppData\\Local\\Temp\\*" OR Filesystem.file_path="*\\AppData\\Roaming\\*" OR Filesystem.file_path="*\\ProgramData\\*" OR Filesystem.file_path="*\\Users\\Public\\*") by Filesystem.dest Filesystem.file_path Filesystem.file_name Filesystem.process_name _time span=5m | `drop_dm_object_name(Filesystem)` | join type=inner dest file_path [ | tstats summariesonly=true count values(Processes.process) as exec_cmdline values(Processes.parent_process_name) as parent from datamodel=Endpoint.Processes where Processes.process_path="*\\AppData\\Local\\Temp\\*" OR Processes.process_path="*\\AppData\\Roaming\\*" OR Processes.process_path="*\\ProgramData\\*" OR Processes.process_path="*\\Users\\Public\\*" by Processes.dest Processes.process_path Processes.process_name | `drop_dm_object_name(Processes)` | rex field=process_path "(?<file_path>.*)\\\\[^\\\\]+$" ] | convert ctime(firstTime) ctime(lastTime)
ucs.kql:
// Tonto Team — DLL side-loading: signed loader exe + adjacent malicious DLL
// in user-writable path (Bisonal / Dexbia / ShadowPad signature)
let _userWritable = dynamic([
    @"\appdata\local\temp\", @"\appdata\roaming\",
    @"\programdata\",        @"\users\public\",
    @"\perflogs\"
]);
let _loaders = DeviceImageLoadEvents
    | where Timestamp > ago(14d)
    | where FileName endswith ".dll"
    | extend FolderLower = tolower(FolderPath),
             InitFolderLower = tolower(InitiatingProcessFolderPath)
    // signed legitimate exe living in a user-writable path
    | where InitFolderLower has_any (_userWritable)
    // DLL is in the SAME directory as the loader exe (side-load)
    | where FolderLower == InitFolderLower
    // exclude DLLs signed by the same vendor as the loader
    | where InitiatingProcessVersionInfoCompanyName !=  ""
    | project Timestamp, DeviceId, DeviceName,
              LoaderExe   = InitiatingProcessFileName,
              LoaderPath  = InitiatingProcessFolderPath,
              LoaderSHA256 = InitiatingProcessSHA256,
              LoaderSigner = InitiatingProcessVersionInfoCompanyName,
              LoadedDll   = FileName,
              DllSHA256   = SHA256;
_loaders
// Pivot: the DLL was created on disk recently (not part of the install media)
| join kind=inner (
    DeviceFileEvents
    | where Timestamp > ago(14d)
    | where ActionType in ("FileCreated","FileRenamed","FileModified")
    | where FileName endswith ".dll"
    | project DropTime = Timestamp, DeviceId,
              LoadedDll = FileName,
              DropProcess = InitiatingProcessFileName,
              DropProcessCmd = InitiatingProcessCommandLine
  ) on DeviceId, LoadedDll
| where DropTime <= Timestamp and DropTime > Timestamp - 7d
// First-time-seen anti-baseline by org
| join kind=leftanti (
    DeviceImageLoadEvents
    | where Timestamp between (ago(60d) .. ago(14d))
    | where FileName endswith ".dll"
    | summarize by InitiatingProcessSHA256, FileName
  ) on $left.LoaderSHA256 == $right.InitiatingProcessSHA256, $left.LoadedDll == $right.FileName
| project Timestamp, DeviceName, LoaderExe, LoaderPath, LoaderSigner,
          LoaderSHA256, LoadedDll, DllSHA256, DropTime, DropProcess, DropProcessCmd
| order by Timestamp desc
ucs.sentinel_kql:
// Tonto Team — DLL side-loading via signed loader in user-writable path
// Sentinel ASIM ImFileEvent + ImProcessCreate (cross-vendor)
let _userWritable = dynamic([
    @"\appdata\local\temp\", @"\appdata\roaming\",
    @"\programdata\",        @"\users\public\",
    @"\perflogs\"
]);
let _dllDrops = ImFileEvent
    | where TimeGenerated > ago(14d)
    | where EventType in ("FileCreated","FileModified")
    | where TargetFileName endswith ".dll"
    | extend PathLower = tolower(TargetFilePath)
    | where PathLower has_any (_userWritable)
    | project DropTime = TimeGenerated, DvcHostname, TargetFileName,
              TargetFilePath, DropProcess = ActingProcessName,
              DropCmd = ActingProcessCommandLine, DllSHA256 = TargetFileSHA256;
let _exeRuns = ImProcessCreate
    | where TimeGenerated > ago(14d)
    | where ActorUsername !endswith "$"
    | extend PathLower = tolower(TargetProcessName)
    | where PathLower has_any (_userWritable)
    | where TargetProcessName endswith ".exe"
    | project ExecTime = TimeGenerated, DvcHostname,
              LoaderExe = TargetProcessName,
              LoaderCmd = TargetProcessCommandLine,
              LoaderSigner = ActingProcessFileCompany,
              ParentProc = ParentProcessName;
_dllDrops
| join kind=inner _exeRuns on DvcHostname
| where ExecTime between (DropTime .. DropTime + 7d)
// Same directory: DLL filename appears in same folder as exe
| extend DropDir = tolower(strcat(TargetFilePath))
| extend ExeDir  = tolower(LoaderExe)
| where indexof(ExeDir, replace_string(DropDir, TargetFileName, "")) >= 0
| project DropTime, ExecTime, DvcHostname, LoaderExe, LoaderSigner,
          DllDropped = TargetFileName, DllPath = TargetFilePath,
          DllSHA256, DropProcess, DropCmd, LoaderCmd
| order by ExecTime desc
ucs.rationale: Tonto Team's post-exploit toolkit (Bisonal RAT, Dexbia, ShadowPad) is consistently deployed via DLL search-order hijacking — Kaspersky, Trend Micro (Earth Akhlut report), Secureworks (BRONZE HUNTLEY), and CrowdStrike (Karma Panda) all document the same pattern: a legitimate signed binary (Microsoft/Kaspersky/vendor utility) dropped to %APPDATA% or %ProgramData% with an adjacent attacker DLL named
